input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware consists of lower and upper chassis levels plus autonomy elements (perception, planning, actuation). The vehicle runs an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) function that is the focus of the digital‑twin study, providing a realistic but resource‑constrained platform for off‑board computation experiments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface streams real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and enables users to view, save, and generate reports; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle identification, desired‑speed calculation) is executed on the DT (e.g., cloud) and the resulting speed command is sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators and developers can remotely enable/disable ACC, set a target speed, and issue an emergency‑braking command via the UI, all supported by low‑latency bi‑directional communication and data‑collection/reporting capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation uses Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) as the middleware for message passing, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for edge‑compute integration, application modules written in Python 3.8 and C++, and model‑based components developed in MATLAB/Simulink. These tools provide the software stack, communication framework, and development environment needed to realize the DT services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT requires clock synchronization between devices with an accuracy of 100 ms, as hardware limits prevent tighter synchronization; this aligns with many safety‑critical vehicular standards that demand sub‑100 ms sync. Synchronization is needed for computation offloading and coordinated operation of the Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based reference model and instantiates three separate entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each deployed on distinct hardware components. Functional Entities (FEs) are mapped to ROS2 packages, yielding a decentralized deployment where each entity hosts its own set of ROS2 nodes. This hierarchical structure allows multiple instances of the same FE (e.g., several sensor‑processing nodes) while maintaining a single logical twin per vehicle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration is realized by mapping each Functional Entity to an individual ROS2 package; the packages contain one or more ROS2 nodes that communicate over DDS. The overall system follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, with the Physical Twin Entity handling resource‑specific functions (point‑cloud clustering, tracking, actuation), the Digital Twin Entity providing data management and control, and the User Entity exposing a 3‑D visualization and control‑panel UI. Coordination across entities is achieved through ROS2 topics/services, enabling runtime resource management and component integration across the three hardware platforms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system organizes its computational models and data within ROS2 packages that constitute Functional Entities (FEs). The Digital Twin Entity hosts data‑management and control software components, including a dedicated Data Storage component. Model functionality includes automotive‑specific algorithms such as Point Cloud Clustering and Tracking, which operate on sensor data collected by the Physical Twin Entity. Visualization of model outputs is provided by the User Interface FE through a 3D Visualization component. All ROS2 packages contain one or more ROS2 nodes that implement these models and manage data flow between entities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation functionality resides in the Physical Twin Entity and is organized as functional entities implemented as ROS 2 nodes inside ROS 2 packages. These nodes expose interfaces that translate high‑level control commands (e.g., desired speed or steering angle) into hardware‑specific signals for vehicle actuators such as motor drives and steering servos. Remote control is performed via the ROS 2 communication layer; the documents do not enumerate specific actuator hardware models or detailed actuation ranges.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing capabilities are encapsulated in the Physical Twin Entity. Sensor acquisition, preprocessing, and device communication are modeled as functional entities implemented as ROS 2 nodes. The architecture acquires raw LiDAR point‑cloud data, which is fed into the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking model for further processing. No additional sensor types, sampling frequencies, or accuracy specifications are provided in the excerpts.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Time synchronization is a key validity requirement for the twin; devices must share a common notion of time with an accuracy of at least 100 ms, imposed by hardware limitations. While stricter synchronization (sub‑100 ms) is mandated by safety‑critical vehicular standards, the system adopts the 100 ms bound to balance feasibility and safety. No further model fidelity, validation, or uncertainty quantification methods are described.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point clouds, motor status, steering servo positions, vehicle state) is streamed to the Digital Twin System. Raw LiDAR point clouds are first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component (Data Pre‑Processing FE). All data streams are then passed to the ISO‑23247 Data Translation functional entity, which serialises the data, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Inter‑component communication inside the DT uses a ROS 2 middleware network, while communication between the physical twin entity and the digital twin entity travels over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. The document does not specify transmission frequency, event triggers, detailed quality‑assurance procedures or integration mechanisms beyond the ROS 2 topics and Data Translation step.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin computes ACC control outputs (e.g., desired speed, enable/disable ACC, emergency‑brake command) and sends them back to the physical twin. Commands are issued from the Controlling FE / User Interface FE to the Actuation FE, which drives the vehicle actuators (motor, steering servo). The messages are transmitted over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link and are handled within the ROS 2 network of the DT. The documents do not detail the exact command message format, validation/authentication mechanisms, conflict‑resolution logic, or explicit execution acknowledgements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS comprises two communication layers: (1) an intra‑system ROS2 (DDS) network that interconnects all software components (Data Translation, Point Cloud Clustering, Controlling FE, etc.) and (2) an inter‑entity Wi‑Fi link that transports data between the physical twin and the digital twin using UDP at the transport layer. No quantitative bandwidth or latency values are given; however, Document 2 notes that the system must support low end‑to‑end latency communication to satisfy safety‑critical requirements. The documents do not mention redundancy mechanisms, security protocols (e.g., encryption, authentication), or specific network topology beyond the client‑server style flow implied by UI → Controlling FE → Wi‑Fi → Physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DT services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) are intended to run on equipment that can provide higher computational resources than the on‑board ECU, with the example of offloading the ACC logic to the cloud. The documents do not specify the concrete hosting platform (cloud provider, edge node, on‑premise server), the required CPU/memory/storage capacities, scalability mechanisms, deployment models (container, VM, etc.) or availability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system is built according to the ISO 23247 digital‑twin framework for manufacturing. It adopts the ISO 23247 reference architecture, defining entities such as the Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity, and implements Functional Entities as ROS 2 packages. For temporal coordination the design follows safety‑critical vehicular standards that require clock synchronization tighter than 100 ms; the implementation enforces a 100 ms accuracy bound as dictated by those standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the physical twin as a 1/10-scale vehicle, details its construction (retrofitted teleoperated vehicle), hardware structure (lower/upper chassis, autonomy elements), and its focus on ACC for resource-constrained experimentation. Supported by quotes: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'built off a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle [3] by retrofitting it with autonomy components', and 'physical architecture consists of three layers... lower level chassis... upper level chassis... and the autonomy elements'.

The extraction comprehensively lists the three core services—Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management—describing their specific functions (e.g., viewing tracks, computing desired speed, emergency braking) as described in the text. Supported by quotes: 'Data Monitoring: Monitoring ACC-relevant data... user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'Computation Offloading... offloading only the ACC system... determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed', and 'Remote Management... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction lists the correct tools and their roles as described in the source: Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as middleware, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for the Jetson Orin Nano, Python 3.8 and C++ for application modules, and MATLAB/Simulink for complex systems. It accurately reflects the provided software stack details. Supporting quotes: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for programming the Jetson Orin Nano', and 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8..., C++..., and MATLAB/Simulink...'

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific synchronization requirement of 100 ms and attributes it correctly to hardware limitations. It also correctly links this synchronization to the need for computation offloading. The extraction slightly misinterprets the relationship to safety standards (the text states standards are stricter/sub-100ms, while the system is 100ms), but the core system constraints are well-represented. Quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.', 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.'

The description correctly identifies the three entities (Physical, Digital, User) and their deployment on distinct hardware. It accurately captures the mapping of Functional Entities to ROS2 packages and the decentralized nature of the architecture. The claim about 'multiple instances of the same FE' is a minor inference not explicitly supported by the text, but the hierarchical description is grounded. Quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component...', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package.', 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity... Digital Twin Entity... Physical Twin Entity.'

The extraction provides a highly specific and accurate breakdown of the system's orchestration, including the mapping of FEs to ROS2 packages and the specific responsibilities of each entity (e.g., point-cloud clustering for Physical, ACC for Digital). It includes the unsupported claim that nodes 'communicate over DDS' (external knowledge, not in text) and 'runtime resource management', but the architectural detail is otherwise excellent. Quotes: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes', 'Physical Twin Entity... Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking... Actuation', 'Digital Twin Entity... Data Collection... ACC... User Entity... 3D Visualization'

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses exclusively on the internal architecture (Physical, Digital, and User Entities) and internal communication standards (ROS2, ISO 23247). It mentions 'cloud-based fleet coordination' only as a general motivation in the abstract, but does not describe specific integration protocols, external system dependencies, or interoperability with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems for the case study implementation.

_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)

processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately details the organization of models and data into ROS2 packages and Functional Entities (FEs), correctly mapping them to the ISO 23247 entities. It correctly identifies the Point Cloud Clustering algorithm, the Data Storage component, and the 3D Visualization within the User Interface FE, along with the implementation details using ROS2 nodes.

The extraction provides a good description of the actuation functionality, mapping it to the Physical Twin Entity and ROS2 nodes. It correctly describes the conversion of high-level commands to hardware signals. However, it incorrectly states that the documents do not enumerate specific actuator hardware models, whereas the source text lists a 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller'. Despite this minor inaccuracy, the technical details regarding the architecture and control flow are well-captured. Quotes: 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.', 'Physical Twin Entity... Actuation FE', 'The DTS shall actuate the ego vehicle in response to user commands and ACC control.'

This characteristic is highly specific and accurate regarding the sensing architecture. It correctly identifies the Physical Twin Entity as the host for sensing capabilities, implemented as ROS2 nodes. It accurately describes the acquisition of LiDAR data and its processing by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking model. It also correctly notes the lack of sampling frequencies and accuracy specs. Quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN', 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'We implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package.'

The extraction precisely captures the time synchronization requirements (R8), including the 100 ms accuracy, the comparison to stricter safety-critical standards, and the rationale regarding hardware limitations. It correctly notes that other fidelity or uncertainty quantification methods are not described in the text.

interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to

(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced

(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements

of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**
